claims.claim: In all three chemistries (NCA, LCO, LMO), ignition time decreased with increasing SOC, indicating lower thermal stability of electroactive materials at higher SOC.
claims.quote: all chemistries showed a decrease in ignition time with the increase in SOC, indicating lower thermal stability of electroactive materials at higher SOC.

claims.claim: TRonset decreased with increasing SOC in all three chemistries (NCA, LCO, LMO), with NCA decreasing from 171.5°C to 146.9°C, LCO from 173.9°C to 142.3°C, and LMO from 192.5°C across 25% to 100% SOC.
claims.quote: it is apparent that in all chemistries, TRonset decreased with the increase in SOC. For instance, from 25% to 100% SOC. TRonset decreased from 171.5°C to 146.9°C, and 192.5°C for NCA, LCO, and LMO LIBs, respectively.

claims.claim: Among the three chemistries tested, LMO showed the lowest TRonset values at all SOC levels, which is associated with its quicker failure and shorter burning time.
claims.quote: Among the three chemistries, LMO showed the lowest TRonset values in all SOCs. This justifies its quicker failure and shorter burning time, as depicted in Fig. S2c.

claims.claim: Safety vent crack in LCO occurred when battery surface temperature reached between 155°C and 185°C, in NCA between 135°C and 165°C, and in LMO between 145°C and 175°C.
claims.quote: the safety vent crack was observed to occur when battery surface temperatures reached between 155°C - 185°C; 135°C - 165°C; and 145°C - 175°C for LCO, NCA, and LMO, respectively.

claims.claim: The peak flame temperature at 20 mm above the LIB is larger than typical LIB gaps in arrays or modules, implying neighboring LIBs may be ignited in this type of burning.
claims.quote: The temperature at the top of LIB (20 mm) was the highest in relation to the other positions. At this position the thermocouple was mainly covered by the hottest flame region. This distance (20 mm) is larger than typical LIB gaps in arrays or modules, and so neighboring LIBs may be inflamed in this type of burning.

claims.claim: The empirical equations for predicting TRonset achieved a percentage error of less than 10% for both NCA and LCO LIBs, with the largest discrepancy at lower SOC (maximum %Er of 7.4% for NCA and 2.7% for LCO at 20% SOC).
claims.quote: The %Er ranges from 0.6% to 7.4% and is independent of the SOC. The largest disparity is seen in the lower SOC, with maximum %Er of 2.7 and 7.4% in LCO and NCA, respectively.

claims.claim: The empirical equations for TRonset, Tsurf, and Tflame are applicable only for NCA and LCO cathode chemistries with 18650 form factors externally heated 2 cm from the heater in an open chamber with no forced air, and are not applicable to LMO due to violent explosions.
claims.quote: the empirical equations developed are found to be applicable for NCA and LCO cathodic chemistries with 18650 form factors externally heated 2 cm from the heater in an open chamber with no forced air... LMO is not taken into account because of the violent explosions observed in many of its samples, which could cause a large discrepancy in the prediction.

claims.claim: FSN during venting increased with SOC up to 100% SOC in NCA and LCO, but during explosion the FSN uptrend reversed after 50% SOC due to violent explosion effects.
claims.quote: During the venting process, the FSN in NCA and LCO LIBs increased with SOC up to 100% SOC, indicating that SOC has an effect on FSN. However, during the explosion, the FSN showed an uptrend up to 50% SOC, after which the FSN values were lowered by the violent explosion.

claims.claim: LCO with 100% SOC stored 9.01 Wh and did not burst the canister, whereas LMO with 100% SOC stored 12.34 Wh and did burst the canister, demonstrating that energy content determines canister rupture behavior across chemistries.
claims.quote: LCO with 100% SOC has 9.01 Wh and did not burst the canister, but LMO with 100% SOC has 12.34 Wh and did break the canister.

claims.claim: Thermal runaway was not observed when the LIB was aligned vertically with the anode terminal facing the horizontally aligned heater, because only a small surface was heated and the received thermal stress was insufficient to trigger TR.
claims.quote: TR was not observed when the LIB under test was aligned vertically with anode terminal facing the horizontally aligned heater. The major reason is that only a small LIB's surface was heated, and the received thermal stress was not enough to trigger TR.
